refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR bundles three distinct obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate functional concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies a specific DP) and focuses on the core problem: making spatial features available in a structured, queryable form. Splitting into atomic sub-FRs allows independent verification and design of each capability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Confirm that a worksite model exists in a defined schema/format, contains spatial coordinates and feature classifications, and is accessible to downstream planning and execution functions per acceptance criteria.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface properties across the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and locate static obstacles (structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent designated work zones (operational areas, restricted zones, safety perimeters) in the worksite model.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Persist and retrieve worksite spatial data in a format accessible to planning and execution functions.
refined_fr.note: The parent FR is now atomic in intent (single obligation: represent spatial features). The sub-FRs are the atomic decomposition that should each map to distinct DPs and IMs.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data via multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety zone) queryable by planning/execution functions.

DP2: Decompose worksite into discrete zones; assign feature capture responsibility to zone-specific acquisition modules; merge zone models via spatial registration and conflict resolution; expose unified model through a feature query interface.

DP3: Implement a real-time streaming pipeline that ingests raw sensor observations, applies incremental feature extraction and classification, and maintains a versioned spatial database; provide read-only snapshots and change notifications to downstream consumers.

DP4: Establish a declarative feature schema (geometry primitives, safety attributes, operational constraints) and enforce capture/representation conformance through schema validation; decouple feature acquisition from model storage via an intermediate normalization layer.

DP5: Delegate feature acquisition to independent sensor subsystems (each with local processing); aggregate outputs via a central spatial reconciliation service that resolves overlaps, validates consistency against safety/operational policies, and publishes a canonical model.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4 + DP5 (hybrid): Establish a declarative feature schema that defines geometry, classification, and safety/operational attributes. Delegate feature acquisition to independent sensor subsystems with local processing; aggregate via a central spatial reconciliation service that validates against the schema and safety/operational policies, resolves spatial conflicts, and publishes a canonical, versioned spatial model accessible to planning/execution functions via a query interface.

potential_sources: Schema evolution: Changes to feature schema may require updates across all sensor subsystems and reconciliation logic; mitigate via schema versioning and backward compatibility.
Spatial registration: Errors in coordinate frame alignment between sensor subsystems can propagate to the unified model; mitigate via explicit registration validation and uncertainty quantification.

Policy enforcement: Safety and operational policies embedded in reconciliation logic may create tight coupling to policy definition; mitigate via externalizing policies as data (rules engine) rather than hard-coded logic.
Real-time consistency: Concurrent updates from multiple sensor subsystems may create transient inconsistencies; mitigate via explicit conflict resolution strategy and eventual consistency guarantees.
coupling_check.notes: DP4 + DP5 achieves loose coupling by separating schema definition (DP4) from acquisition/aggregation (DP5). Independent sensor subsystems can operate autonomously; the reconciliation service acts as a mediator. Risk is in the reconciliation logic becoming a bottleneck or policy-coupling point; recommend externalizing policies as queryable rules.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Sensor subsystems can operate independently and produce feature observations in a common coordinate frame (or with sufficient metadata for registration). Risk: If sensors are tightly coupled (e.g., require real-time synchronization), independence is compromised.

Assumption: Feature schema is stable enough to support multiple sensor subsystems without frequent breaking changes. Risk: Evolving safety/operational policies may require schema changes; recommend versioning strategy.

Assumption: Spatial reconciliation can be performed offline or with acceptable latency (not hard real-time). Risk: If planning/execution requires sub-100ms model updates, streaming DP3 may be more suitable.

Assumption: Worksite features are well-defined and classifiable (e.g., obstacles, hazard zones, access points). Risk: Ambiguous or novel features may fail schema validation; recommend fallback handling (e.g., 'unknown' classification with manual review).

Assumption: Safety and operational policies are expressible as constraints on spatial features (e.g., 'no machinery within 2m of excavation'). Risk: Complex, context-dependent policies may require domain-specific reasoning beyond spatial queries.

Assumption: Planning/execution functions can tolerate eventual consistency (model updates lag real-world changes by seconds to minutes). Risk: If real-time responsiveness is critical, streaming or predictive models may be needed.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) Latency tolerance for model availability (e.g., 'model must be updated within 5 minutes of feature change'), (2) Consistency model (strong vs. eventual), (3) Policy expression format (declarative rules, constraints, or imperative logic), (4) Sensor subsystem independence assumptions (can they operate offline? require synchronization?). This will clarify whether DP4+DP5 (batch/eventual consistency) or DP3 (streaming/strong consistency) is more appropriate. Additionally, introduce an explicit IM layer to map reconciliation service, schema registry, and sensor subsystem boundaries to owned modules/teams.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized spatial reconciliation, schema validation, conflict resolution, canonical spatial model management, and safety/operational policy enforcement for multi-sensor feature integration

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receive raw sensor observations (geometry, classification, attributes) from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Publish safety violations, spatial conflicts, and policy breaches detected during reconciliation

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receive planning/execution queries for canonical spatial model; return versioned, validated spatial state and feature metadata

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Receive telemetry from remote or distributed sensor subsystems if applicable

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: schema validation, conflict resolution, and safety/operational policy enforcement are core safety responsibilities. Spatial reconciliation and canonical model management align with centralized safety oversight. Minor diffusion: sensor-local processing remains distributed (Vehicle/Teleoperation), but aggregation and arbitration are fully contained. Query interface to planning/execution is a clean outbound contract.

Moderate fit: NGHE is a top-level orchestrator; placing spatial reconciliation and schema validation here risks making NGHE a monolithic policy engine rather than a coordinator. Safety-specific logic (policy enforcement, conflict resolution) would diffuse into planning/execution layers. Canonical model would become a global singleton, reducing modularity.

Poor fit: Vehicle is a physical platform with local sensor integration. Centralizing spatial reconciliation and safety policy validation at the vehicle level breaks the abstraction—planning and teleoperation would need to query the vehicle for spatial state, creating tight coupling. Schema and policy management belong at a higher, system-wide level.

Poor fit: Teleoperation is a remote control interface, not a data aggregation or safety arbitration layer. Placing spatial reconciliation here would make teleoperation responsible for system-wide spatial truth, conflating user control with safety validation. High diffusion: planning and autonomous execution would bypass teleoperation to access spatial state.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the natural owner of the declarative feature schema, spatial reconciliation logic, and safety/operational policy validation. It sits at the system level, above individual sensor subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation) and below planning/execution (NGHE). This placement ensures that safety-critical spatial arbitration is centralized, auditable, and decoupled from sensor acquisition details. The canonical spatial model becomes a versioned, queryable artifact managed by Safety Services, with clear contracts (Telemetry in, SafetyAlert and Tasking out).
containment_risks: Risk: If Safety Services becomes a bottleneck for spatial queries, latency-sensitive planning/execution may bypass it or cache stale state. Mitigation: design the query interface (Tasking) with low-latency guarantees and publish versioned snapshots.

Risk: Sensor-local processing (Vehicle, Teleoperation) may not conform to the declared schema, causing frequent validation failures. Mitigation: establish a schema registration and versioning protocol; require sensor subsystems to declare their output schema upfront.

Risk: Spatial conflicts may be unresolvable by policy alone (e.g., two equally valid but contradictory observations). Mitigation: define escalation procedures and human-in-the-loop resolution paths within Safety Services.

Risk: Multiple independent sensor subsystems may produce overlapping or redundant features. Mitigation: the reconciliation service must include deduplication and fusion logic; this is a core responsibility of Safety Services.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP required. The DP is well-suited to Safety Services. However, clarify the DP to explicitly state: (1) Sensor-local processing remains owned by Vehicle and Teleoperation; Safety Services does NOT replicate sensor logic. (2) The declarative schema is a contract published by Safety Services; sensor subsystems must conform or declare non-conformance. (3) The canonical spatial model is versioned and immutable once published; updates trigger new versions, not in-place mutations. (4) The query interface (Tasking) includes a timestamp/version parameter to allow planning/execution to request specific spatial snapshots for reproducibility.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4 + DP5 (hybrid) directly addresses FR by establishing a declarative feature schema (geometry, classification, safety/operational attributes) and a central spatial reconciliation service that aggregates sensor observations, validates against schema and policies, resolves conflicts, and publishes a canonical spatial model. The DP is solution-complete: it specifies both the logical structure (schema, reconciliation logic, policy enforcement) and the operational flow (sensor input → validation → conflict resolution → canonical model → query interface). This satisfies the FR's requirement to capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model accessible to planning/execution.

diffusion_risks: Minor diffusion: Sensor-local processing (Vehicle, Teleoperation subsystems) remains distributed; Safety Services does not own sensor acquisition. This is intentional and correct, but requires clear schema contracts to prevent validation failures. Mitigation: establish schema registration and versioning protocol upfront.

Latency bottleneck risk: If Safety Services becomes a synchronous gatekeeper for all spatial queries, latency-sensitive planning/execution may bypass it or cache stale state. Mitigation: design Tasking query interface with low-latency guarantees (e.g., sub-100ms response) and publish versioned snapshots to reduce query load.

Unresolvable conflict risk: Spatial conflicts may exceed policy-based resolution (e.g., two equally valid but contradictory sensor observations). Mitigation: define escalation procedures and human-in-the-loop resolution paths within Safety Services; document conflict resolution precedence rules.

Deduplication and fusion complexity: Multiple independent sensor subsystems may produce overlapping or redundant features. This is a core responsibility of Safety Services but requires sophisticated fusion logic. Mitigation: implement feature deduplication by spatial proximity and classification; define fusion rules in the declarative schema.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Safety Services is the optimal IM owner (containment_score: 0.92, diffusion_score: 0.08). The module sits at the system level, above individual sensor subsystems and below planning/execution, ensuring safety-critical spatial arbitration is centralized, auditable, and decoupled from sensor details. The four external interfaces (Telemetry in, SafetyAlert out, Tasking inout, Network Link in) are clean and well-defined. Schema validation, conflict resolution, and safety/operational policy enforcement are core safety responsibilities. Canonical spatial model management and versioning are appropriate for a centralized safety service. Alternative placements (NGHE, Vehicle, Teleoperation) all score poorly (0.35–0.65 containment) due to architectural misalignment: NGHE would become a monolithic policy engine; Vehicle would create tight coupling with planning; Teleoperation would conflate user control with safety validation.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criteria are concrete and measurable: (1) 'A worksite model/map is generated and available to planning/execution functions for an agreed set of site features' is verifiable by querying the Tasking interface and confirming that Safety Services returns a canonical spatial model containing all agreed features with valid geometry, classification, and attributes. (2) 'Worksite safety plan and operational policies' are verifiable by confirming that Safety Services enforces declared policies (e.g., no-go zones, hazard buffers) and publishes SafetyAlert messages when violations are detected. Test cases: (a) inject sensor observations via Telemetry; verify canonical model is updated and queryable; (b) inject conflicting observations; verify conflict resolution and SafetyAlert publication; (c) query Tasking with version/timestamp parameters; verify reproducible spatial snapshots.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR is allocated to Safety Services via DP4 + DP5. Ownership boundaries are explicit: Safety Services owns schema definition, reconciliation logic, policy enforcement, and canonical model management. Sensor subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation) own local processing and observation generation; they are consumers of the schema contract, not owners of reconciliation. Planning/execution (NGHE) owns query logic and spatial reasoning; it is a consumer of the canonical model via the Tasking interface. Interfaces are well-defined: Telemetry (in), SafetyAlert (out), Tasking (inout), Network Link (in). No ambiguity about which module is responsible for which aspect of spatial feature capture and representation.

recommended_revisions: Clarify DP to explicitly state: (1) Sensor-local processing remains owned by Vehicle and Teleoperation; Safety Services does NOT replicate or override sensor logic. (2) The declarative schema is a contract published by Safety Services; sensor subsystems must conform or declare non-conformance upfront. (3) The canonical spatial model is versioned and immutable once published; updates trigger new versions, not in-place mutations. (4) The Tasking query interface includes a timestamp/version parameter to allow planning/execution to request specific spatial snapshots for reproducibility and auditability.

Define schema registration and versioning protocol: Require sensor subsystems to declare their output schema (geometry types, classification taxonomy, attribute ranges) before integration. Establish a schema versioning mechanism (e.g., semantic versioning) to manage evolution without breaking planning/execution consumers.

Specify conflict resolution precedence rules: Document how Safety Services prioritizes conflicting observations (e.g., most recent, highest confidence, majority vote, human escalation). Define escalation procedures for unresolvable conflicts and establish human-in-the-loop resolution paths.

Design Tasking query interface for low-latency access: Specify response time guarantees (e.g., <100ms for canonical model queries) to prevent planning/execution from bypassing Safety Services. Consider publish-subscribe patterns (e.g., versioned snapshots published on a schedule) to reduce query load.

Implement feature deduplication and fusion logic: Define spatial proximity thresholds and classification matching rules to identify and merge redundant features from multiple sensors. Document fusion precedence (e.g., which sensor observation takes priority if two sensors report the same feature with different attributes).

Establish SafetyAlert contract: Specify alert severity levels, message format, and delivery guarantees (e.g., at-least-once, ordered by timestamp). Define which policy violations trigger alerts and which are logged without alerting.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The DP is solution-complete and the IM (Safety Services) is architecturally sound with clear ownership and interfaces. However, the recommended_revisions expose critical sub-level design decisions that must be decomposed to ensure safety-critical correctness and operational robustness. Specifically: (1) Schema registration and versioning protocol is a non-trivial sub-system requiring its own FR-DP-IM analysis; (2) Conflict resolution precedence rules and escalation procedures are safety-critical policies that must be formally specified and verified; (3) Feature deduplication and fusion logic is a complex algorithmic responsibility that warrants explicit functional decomposition; (4) Tasking query interface latency guarantees and publish-subscribe patterns are architectural decisions affecting system performance and safety. Given high safety criticality, decomposing these sub-concerns now prevents downstream integration failures and ensures each sub-component is independently verifiable and auditable.
next_level_sub_frs: FR4.1: Establish and maintain a declarative schema contract that defines geometry types, classification taxonomy, attribute ranges, and versioning semantics for all sensor observations integrated into the canonical spatial model.
FR4.2: Reconcile conflicting spatial observations by applying declared precedence rules (recency, confidence, majority vote, human escalation) and publish resolution decisions with audit trail.

FR4.3: Deduplicate and fuse redundant spatial features from multiple independent sensors using spatial proximity thresholds and classification matching rules, producing a single canonical representation per feature.

FR4.4: Enforce worksite safety and operational policies (no-go zones, hazard buffers, access restrictions) against the canonical spatial model and publish SafetyAlert messages with severity levels and delivery guarantees when violations are detected.

FR4.5: Provide a low-latency, versioned query interface (Tasking) that allows planning/execution to retrieve canonical spatial snapshots with timestamp/version parameters for reproducibility and auditability, with response time guarantees <100ms.

revise.why: The current DP4 + DP5 description is solution-complete at the logical level but lacks explicit specification of five critical sub-design decisions: (1) schema registration/versioning protocol (DP4.1), (2) conflict resolution precedence and escalation (DP4.2), (3) feature deduplication/fusion algorithm (DP4.3), (4) policy enforcement and SafetyAlert contract (DP4.4), (5) Tasking query interface design with latency guarantees (DP4.5). Revise DP to reference these sub-DPs as mandatory design elaborations, not optional mitigations. This ensures the DP is traceable to verifiable sub-components and prevents ambiguity during implementation.

assumptions: Safety Services has sufficient computational capacity to perform real-time schema validation, conflict resolution, and feature fusion without introducing unacceptable latency (<100ms query response).
Sensor subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation) can be contractually bound to conform to the declared schema; non-conformance is detectable and escalatable.
Conflict resolution precedence rules can be defined deterministically for the majority of spatial conflicts; unresolvable conflicts are rare enough to justify human-in-the-loop escalation.
Planning/execution (NGHE) will not bypass Safety Services for spatial queries; architectural enforcement (e.g., no direct sensor access) is feasible.
Network Link provides reliable, ordered delivery of Telemetry observations; out-of-order or dropped messages are handled gracefully by Safety Services (e.g., via versioning and idempotency).
open_risks: Schema evolution risk: As sensor subsystems are added or upgraded, schema changes may break existing planning/execution consumers. Mitigation requires a robust versioning and deprecation strategy; this is addressed in FR4.1 but must be validated during sub-level design.

Fusion complexity risk: Feature deduplication and fusion logic may become intractable for high-dimensional sensor data (e.g., LiDAR point clouds, thermal imagery). Mitigation requires algorithmic research and prototyping; FR4.3 must include feasibility gates.

Latency bottleneck risk: If Safety Services becomes a synchronous gatekeeper, planning/execution may experience unacceptable delays. Mitigation via publish-subscribe and caching (FR4.5) must be validated with realistic workload models.

Unresolvable conflict escalation risk: Human-in-the-loop resolution may introduce delays or inconsistencies if escalation procedures are not clearly defined. FR4.2 must specify escalation SLAs and decision authority.
SafetyAlert delivery risk: If SafetyAlert messages are lost or delayed, safety violations may go undetected. FR4.4 must specify delivery guarantees (at-least-once, ordered) and monitoring mechanisms.
evidence_needed: Schema registration protocol specification: Document the process by which sensor subsystems declare their output schema, including validation rules, versioning semantics, and deprecation procedures. Include examples for common sensor types (e.g., Vehicle odometry, Teleoperation camera feed).

Conflict resolution decision tree: Enumerate representative conflict scenarios (e.g., two sensors report same feature with different geometry, two sensors report overlapping but distinct features, sensor reports feature that contradicts policy). For each, specify the precedence rule and expected outcome. Include test cases.

Feature deduplication algorithm specification: Define spatial proximity thresholds (e.g., <0.5m Euclidean distance), classification matching rules (e.g., exact match, fuzzy match with confidence threshold), and fusion logic (e.g., average geometry, union of attributes). Include computational complexity analysis and performance benchmarks.

Policy enforcement specification: Document the set of safety and operational policies (e.g., no-go zones, hazard buffers, access restrictions) and how they are represented in the schema. Specify the evaluation logic and SafetyAlert trigger conditions. Include examples.

Tasking query interface specification: Define the API contract (e.g., REST, gRPC, message queue), query parameters (e.g., spatial bounds, feature types, timestamp/version), response format, and latency guarantees. Include load testing results demonstrating <100ms response time under realistic query rates.

Integration test plan: Specify test scenarios for each sub-FR, including sensor observation injection, conflict injection, policy violation injection, and query load testing. Include acceptance criteria and pass/fail thresholds.
